title: How breathwork can reopen what talk therapy sealed shut
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:
I thought talk therapy was the answer 

 I spent years on couches. Therapist after therapist. Nodding heads, soft voices, boxes of tissues. I told my stor...
htmlContent:
I thought talk therapy was the answer

I spent years on couches. Therapist after therapist. Nodding heads, soft voices, boxes of tissues. I told my story so many times I could recite it in my sleep. The childhood stuff. The betrayals. The moments that split me into before and after.

And it helped. Sort of. I learned words for what happened. I stopped blaming myself for things that weren't my fault. I could explain my triggers, my patterns, my defenses. I felt seen, heard, understood.

But my body didn't get the memo.

Here's the thing. Talk therapy works for the thinking brain. The part that makes meaning, constructs narratives, builds insight. That's valuable. I'm not here to trash therapy. But there's a problem nobody warned me about. Sometimes talk therapy seals things shut. It creates a neat story about your pain without ever touching the actual wound.

And then breathwork came along and ripped the sutures open.

I'm not being dramatic. I mean that literally. After years of feeling like I had processed my trauma, I took a breathwork class and found myself sobbing on a yoga mat, my body shaking, memories surfacing that I thought were long gone. My therapist had helped me build a safe narrative. My breathwork practice showed me the parts of that narrative that were lies.

Stay with me here. I need you to understand the difference between talking about trauma and experiencing it in your body. Because that difference might save you years of confusion.

What talk therapy can't reach

Bessel van der Kolk spent decades studying how trauma lives in the body. His research at the Trauma Center in Boston showed something unsettling. People who could tell a coherent story about their trauma often still had physical symptoms. Panic attacks. Chronic pain. Digestive issues. That strange feeling of being disconnected from their own skin.

Something happens in the body when safety has been absent for a long time. The muscles forget how to relax. Not metaphorically - literally. The fascia tightens. The diaphragm restricts. The psoas - that deep hip flexor that connects your spine to your legs - stays chronically contracted, ready to run or curl into a ball at any moment.

Bessel van der Kolk's research on yoga and trauma showed something worth noticing. Traumatized people who practiced yoga showed changes in their brain scans - specifically in the insula, the part of the brain responsible for interoception, for feeling what's happening inside your own body. Many trauma survivors have learned to disconnect from body sensation entirely. It was too dangerous to feel. Yoga - gentle, trauma-informed yoga - helped them come back.

I'm not saying yoga fixes everything. I'm saying the body needs to be included in healing. You can't think your way out of something that lives below thought. You have to feel your way through it. Slowly. With support. At your own pace. There's no rushing this.

Talk therapy gave them a narrative. It didn't give them a body.

Here's what happens when you talk about trauma. Your prefrontal cortex lights up. That's the part of your brain that makes sense of things. You construct a timeline. You assign meaning. You create distance between the event and yourself. That distance can feel like healing. But it's often just a coping strategy dressed up as insight.

Meanwhile, your nervous system is still holding the original activation. The fear, the helplessness, the rage you couldn't express. That stuff doesn't live in your words. It lives in your muscles, your fascia, your breath patterns, your vagus nerve. It lives in the way your shoulders stay tensed even when you're relaxed. In the way your throat tightens when someone raises their voice. In the way your breath gets shallow when you think about the past.

A book that many trauma survivors find genuinely helpful is The Body Keeps the Score (paid link). Van der Kolk lays out the science clearly. The body doesn't forget. No matter how many times you tell the story, the body is still waiting for you to finish the incomplete survival responses. The fight that never happened. The flight that was impossible. The freeze that got stuck.

So you talk. And talk. And talk some more. You build a beautiful, coherent narrative. And your body sits there, silently screaming, wondering when you're going to notice.

Breathwork as a direct line to the nervous system

Stephen Porges, the neuroscientist who developed Polyvagal Theory, showed us something crucial. The breath is one of the few things that connects voluntary and involuntary nervous system function. You can't decide to lower your heart rate. You can't command your digestion to work better. But you can change your breath. And when you do, your entire nervous system responds.

This is why breathwork can reach places talk therapy can't. It bypasses the storytelling brain entirely. It goes straight to the brainstem, the vagus nerve, the ancient parts of your nervous system that don't speak English. They speak rhythm. They speak pressure. They speak expansion and contraction.

When I first tried conscious connected breathing, I had no idea what I was getting into. I lay on a mat. The facilitator guided us to breathe in a continuous loop. No pause between inhale and exhale. Just a steady, rhythmic wave of breath moving through the body.

Within ten minutes, my hands started tingling. Then my arms. Then my face. My jaw began to tremble. Tears came without any story attached. No sad memory. No triggering thought. Just pure physical release. My body was finally saying what it had been holding for decades.

Something worth exploring for those drawn to body-based healing is In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) by Peter Levine. Levine founded Somatic Experiencing, and this book explains how the body completes trauma responses through physical sensation rather than narrative. It's a different model entirely. One that trusts the body knows what it needs.

Here's what I wish someone had told me before I started. Breathwork can reopen wounds that talk therapy sealed shut. Not because talk therapy was wrong. But because talk therapy often builds a lid on a pot that's still boiling. The breath turns up the heat. And what comes out can be overwhelming.

Right?

I remember the first time I felt genuine terror during a breathwork session. Not the memory of terror. Not the story of being scared. Actual, present-tense terror in my body. My heart was pounding. My muscles were rigid. I wanted to run. But I was lying down. There was nowhere to go. So I just had to be with it.

That's the part nobody sells you about breathwork. It's not always relaxing. Sometimes it's the hardest thing you'll ever do. But that difficulty is precisely where the healing lives.

Here's something wild. Your body can hold a trauma response for decades without you knowing it. You might think you've "dealt with" something because you can talk about it without crying. But talking about it uses the left hemisphere - language, logic, narrative. The trauma might still be living in your right hemisphere, in your body, in your implicit memory.

Pat Ogden's work on sensorimotor psychotherapy addresses exactly this gap. She noticed that clients could have years of talk therapy and still be triggered by the same things. Because the body hadn't been included in the conversation. The body was still bracing, still guarding, still waiting for the other shoe to drop.

Try this. Stand up for a moment if you can. Feel your feet on the ground. Now push gently against a wall with both hands. Feel the resistance. Feel your own strength pushing back. That's your body remembering that it can take action. That it's not helpless. That it has agency. Sometimes the body needs to complete a movement it was never allowed to make. That's not weird. That's healing.

The risk of reopening without support

I need to be honest with you. This work can go wrong. I've seen it happen. People who've done years of talk therapy decide to try a breathwork workshop. They have a massive release. They feel amazing for a few days. Then they crash. Hard. The old coping mechanisms stop working. The narrative they built in therapy suddenly feels hollow. They're raw, exposed, and don't know what to do.

Janina Fisher, who trained with both van der Kolk and Richard Schwartz, talks about this extensively. She calls it "therapeutic decompensation." When you open something the nervous system wasn't ready to release, you can end up worse than you started. The key, she says, is titration. Small doses. Gradual exposure. Not ripping the bandage off all at once.

So if you're considering breathwork, especially if you have a trauma history, please do it with a trained facilitator. Not a random YouTube video. Not a friend who took a weekend training. Someone who understands trauma. Someone who can help you stay regulated when things get intense.

A weighted blanket that many find calming for their nervous system is YnM Weighted Blanket 15 lbs (paid link). Having something like this available after a session can help your body come back to safety. The deep pressure input signals to your nervous system that you're held, protected, not floating off into space.

I also recommend having earplugs handy. After a deep breathwork session, your nervous system can be hypersensitive to sound for hours. A discreet option for reducing noise sensitivity is Loop Quiet 2 Ear Plugs (paid link). They lower ambient noise without making you feel disconnected from your environment. Small things like this can make the difference between integration and overwhelm.

Integrating the two approaches

I don't think we have to choose between talk therapy and body-based work. I think we need both. But the order matters. And the balance matters.

Here's what I've found works for me and many people I've spoken with. Start with talk therapy to build stability. Create a narrative that makes sense. Develop resources for coping. Learn to recognize when you're dissociating or flooding. Build a relationship with a therapist you trust.

Then, when you have some foundation, introduce body-based work. Slowly. With support. Let the breathwork open what talk therapy prepared you to handle. The narrative you built in therapy becomes a container. The breathwork provides the release. Together, they're more powerful than either alone.

I still do talk therapy. I still need someone to help me make meaning of what comes up in my body. But I also need the breath. I need to feel my ribs expand. I need to let my diaphragm drop. I need to allow my nervous system to complete the cycles it's been stuck in for years.

Think about it. Your body has been breathing since before you were born. It's the most fundamental rhythm of your existence. And it's been shaped by every experience you've ever had. The way you breathe right now, in this moment, is a direct reflection of your nervous system state. Shallow. Tight. Guarded. Or deep. Full. Open.

Changing your breath changes your nervous system. Changing your nervous system changes what your body can hold. And what your body can hold determines what you can actually heal.

Not just talk about. Not just understand. Actually heal.

That's the difference. And it's a difference you can feel. In your chest. In your belly. In the space between one breath and the next.

I'm not saying throw away your therapy. I'm saying your body deserves a seat at the table. And sometimes, the only way to give it that seat is to stop talking and start breathing.

  • The Body Keeps the Score (paid link) - Bessel van der Kolk's foundational work on trauma and the body
  • In an Unspoken Voice (paid link) - Peter Levine on how the body releases trauma
